State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95614

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660           322-3901         322-6441        322-6444

December 16, 1982


D. Dwight Worden
City Attorney
City of Del Mar
1050 Camino del Mar
Del Mar, CA 92014

                                          Re: Our No. A-82-190

Dear Mr. Worden:

     Thank you for your request for advice from our office.
The facts, as I understand them, are that a letter, paid
for by the Sunny Majornier for Assembly Committee, was sent
to registered voters in Ms. Majornier's Assembly District.
The letter bears the seal of the City of del Mar, although
the letter is not on official City letterhead. In the
letter, two Del Mar City councilmembers endorsed
Assemblywoman Majornier. Your question is whether the
letter's format violates any provisions of the law.

     The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a candidate
from sending a letter containing a copy of a city seal.
The only requirement the Political Reform Act imposes on a
mailing, such as the one sent by Ms. Majornier, is that it
truthfully reveal who has actually sent the mailing. In
the case of a mass mailing, which the Act defines as being
two hundred or more identical or nearly identical pieces of
mail (Government Code Section 8204.5),1/ no person can
make an expenditure for the purpose of sending a mass
mailing unless the sender's2/ name, address and city are

_________________________

     1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

     2/ The "sender" is the person who incurs the largest
portion of expenditures attributable to the designing,
printing and posting of the mailing. (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18435.)

D. Dwight Worden
December 16, 1982
Page 2


shown on the outside of each piece of mail in the mass
mailing and on at least one of the inserts included within
each piece of mail3/ (Section 84305).

Assuming that the mailing by the Majoinnier for Assembly
Committee constitutes a mass mailing, it appears likely
that the requirements of Section 84305 were satisfied.
Although you did not send the envelope, you described it as
a "campaign mailer envelope," which leads me to assume that
the sender was identified on it. In addition, the insert
that was inside of the mailing bears the name and address
of the sender.

It does not appear that there are any other applicable
provisions of the Political Reform Act. I suggest that you
contact the Attorney General's Office regarding other laws
that may be relevant. You indicated an interest in the law
concerning the misuse of public funds and so I have
enclosed some information for you. In your letter, you did
not indicate that City funds were used for the Majoinnier
mailing, however, if you do feel that City funds were used,
there are Political Reform Act provisions that apply to the
reporting of such expenditures.

If I can be of any further help to you, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosure

3/ In not less than six-point type.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance                Administration                Executive/Legal              Enforcement              Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                322-5660                    322-5901                322-6441                    322-6444


November 17, 1982


D. Dwight Worden
City Attorney
City of Del Mar
1050 Camino del Mar
Del Mar, CA 92014

                                                         Re: A-82-190

Dear Mr. Worden:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                         Very truly yours,


                                         [illegible]
                                         Barbara A. Milman
                                         Chief
                                         Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

city of del mar

November 12, 1982

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street, P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Sirs:

At its regular meeting of November 1, 1982, the Del
Mar City Council voted unanimously, with Councilwoman Carsten
abstaining, to direct me to seek a letter opinion from your
agency on the following question:

QUESTION: Does the format and layout of the enclosed
letter violate any provision of law?

Specifically, is it improper to make such a political
mailing containing the seal and name of the City of Del Mar?
In the opinion of your agency, is the letter misleading in
that it could be construed as an official City communication?

The following facts may be relevant to your review of
this question:

1. The paper is not on Del Mar City letterhead. A
copy of the City of Del Mar letterhead is enclosed for
comparison.

2. Apparently, the letter was made by photocopying
the business cards of the two councilmembers in question onto
the letter.

3. Apparently, the letter was sent to registered voters
outside the City of Del Mar in the subject assembly district,
although a few Del Mar residents did receive the letter.

4. The letter was mailed by the Mojonnier for Assembly
Committee in a campaign mailer envelope.

Our City Council would greatly appreciate any guidance
you can give us on this matter. In directing my office to
seek this letter opinion from your agency, the Council felt
it was imperative to have a qualified, neutral third party
review this matter and render an opinion. While my office has not
rendered an opinion on the questions presented to your agency, I
have advised the Council as follows:

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Page 2
November 12, 1982


1.  It is illegal to utilize City stationery for
personal or political purposes.

2.  It is improper to "appropriate" the City name, seal
or imprimatur in a way that creates a false or misleading
impression that the communication is official City business
or states an official City position, when in fact, the
communication is personal or political.

4.  Any member of the City Council has the right to
endorse candidates of their choosing and in doing so to
identify themselves as a member of the City Council.

On behalf of the City Council, I express appreciation
for any assistance you can give us.

Sincerely,

[signature]

D. Dwight Worden
City Attorney

DDW/dth

Enclosures

cc: Del Mar City Council
    (w/enclosures)

ARLENE CARSTEN
COUNCILWOMAN

JAMES W. TETRAULT
COUNCILMAN

CITY OF DEL MAR
1050 CAMINO DEL MAR
DEL MAR, CA 92014

Res: (714) 453-0917
Bus: (714) 755-9313

CITY OF DEL MAR
1050 CAMINO DEL MAR
DEL MAR, CA 92014

Res: (714) 481-4541
Bus: (714) 755-9313

DEL MAR CITY COUNCIL MEMBERS

October, 1982

Dear Fellow Concerned Voter:

We are council members in Dick Roe's hometown. We know Dick
Roe. We know Sunny Mojonnier. We are voting for Sunny Mojonnier.

Sunny Mojonnier knows Sacramento. For the last seven years she has
worked in Sacramento on legislation and budgetary matters. She
knows the legislative process.

Sunny is a successful businesswoman in a highly environmentally
sensitive business. She is a flower grower.

Dick Roe led Del Mar in its multi-million dollar law suit against
the City of San Diego. He continued to be a leader in that fiasco
despite losing in the Superior and Appellate courts. When our
City could have received between $1 and $2 million in a settlement
of the case, Roe helped prevent even a discussion of settlement.
On Thursday, October 21, 1982, the Supreme Court unanimously said
Dick Roe's position was wrong. Now, Del Mar gets nothing. This
kind of irresponsible leadership is not what we want in Sacramento.

If that's not enough you should know that many of Roe's ideas are
pretty far out. For example, he has proposed suspending a tube
under the Coronado Bridge to accommodate bicycle and wheelchair
travelers.

We want responsible leadership; we want Sunny Mojonnier representing
us in the State Assembly.

Sincerely,

Arlene Carsten
Councilwoman

James W. Tetrault
Councilman

Paid for by Sunny Mojonnier for Assembly
1348 Governor Dr. #D, San Diego, CA 92122 (714) 450-0666

city of del mar

1050 camino del mar, del mar, california 92014 (714) 755-9313

sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit sending a letter or mass mailing that identifies the sender. However, if a mass mailing contains 200 or more pieces, specific disclosure requirements apply, including identification of the sender on the outside of the mailing and on each piece. Although the disputed letter did not use a campaign-mailer envelope, the identification and format complied with applicable requirements. Additional guidance regarding potential violations of other Political Reform Act provisions and proper reporting of any expenditures may require consultation with the Attorney General.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a political campaign letter containing city council members' business cards and references to the City of Del Mar violates Political Reform Act provisions by potentially appearing to be an official city communication.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that while the letter's use of city council member names and references to Del Mar did not inherently violate the Political Reform Act's disclosure requirements, compliance with mass mailing identification rules was necessary. Further investigation into whether city funds were used and potential misuse of public resources was recommended.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document quality varies with some OCR degradation in the FPPC response letter, but question and conclusion sections are reasonably clear. The response letter (December 16, 1982) provides substantive analysis. Earlier administrative letter (November 17) merely acknowledges receipt. The actual political letter and city attorney's request are clearly reproduced. Some content remains difficult to parse due to document quality.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit sending a letter or mass mailing that identifies the sender. However, if a mass mailing contains 200 or more pieces, specific disclosure requirements apply, including identification of the sender on the outside of the mailing and on each piece. Although the disputed letter did not use a campaign-mailer envelope, the identification and format complied with applicable requirements. Additional guidance regarding potential violations of other Political Reform Act provisions and proper reporting of any expenditures may require consultation with the Attorney General.

The facts, as I understand them, are that a letter, paid for by the Sunny Majornier for Assembly Committee, was sent to registered voters in Ms. Majornier's Assembly District. The letter bears the seal of the City of del Mar, although the letter is not on official City letterhead. In the letter, two Del Mar City councilmembers endorsed Assemblywoman Majornier. Your question is whether the letter's format violates any provisions of the law. The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a candidate from sending a letter containing a copy of a city seal. The only requirement the Political Reform Act imposes on a mailing, such as the one sent by Ms. Majornier, is that it truthfully reveal who has actually sent the mailing. Assuming that the mailing by the Majoinnier for Assembly Committee constitutes a mass mailing, it appears likely that the requirements of Section 84305 were satisfied. Although you did not send the envelope, you described it as a "campaign mailer envelope," which leads me to assume that the sender was identified on it. embedding.summary: The Del Mar City Council sought FPPC guidance on whether two council members' political campaign letter endorsing an assembly candidate violated the Political Reform Act by using council members' names and city references in a way that might appear to be official city communication. The FPPC advised that identification and disclosure requirements apply but that the letter's format did not automatically violate the Act if proper disclosures were made.
